title: The Police Broke In — So the Court Let Her Go
title_alternates: Why Police Can't Use Evidence From an Illegal Search
The Search That Rewrote the Fourth Amendment
Can Police Use Evidence They Took Illegally?
description:
Police forced their way into a Cleveland home without a real warrant — and what they found was thrown out of court. This is Mapp v. Ohio (1961): how one illegal search created the exclusionary rule, the reason American courts throw out evidence obtained by violating the Fourth Amendment — and the controversial cost of keeping the police honest.

⏱ Chapters
0:00 The officers at the door
0:43 The Fourth Amendment's missing piece
1:36 1957 — the warrantless search
3:38 The gap the law forgot (Wolf v. Colorado)
5:54 1961 — the rule changes
8:02 The cost: 'the constable has blundered'
10:14 Rights without remedies

📚 Sources
Mapp v. Ohio, 367 U.S. 643 (1961).
Wolf v. Colorado, 338 U.S. 25 (1949).
Weeks v. United States, 232 U.S. 383 (1914).

ℹ️ About this video
Independent educational documentary. Narration is AI-generated. Historical moments that were never filmed are shown as clearly labeled symbolic reconstructions — not authentic footage. This explains how the law works and is not legal advice.

▶ Previous: who even gets a lawyer? — Gideon v. Wainwright.
▶ Next: who decides what counts as a crime?
👉 Subscribe for the hidden systems behind everyday life.

chapters.title: The gap the law forgot (Wolf v. Colorado)

pinned_comment: Police broke into Dollree Mapp's home without a real warrant — and the evidence was thrown out. The rule that protects you can also free the guilty 'because the constable has blundered.' Worth the trade-off?
